text.title: Survey Design Process and Question Writing Rules
text.summary: Six-step survey design process with question type selection, bias patterns, structure rules, and length guidelines.

rule.statement: Run 10 or more customer interviews before designing a survey. Surveys quantify hypotheses shaped by qualitative research; they do not discover new problems.
rule.rationale: Surveys without a qualitative foundation ask the wrong questions with high statistical precision and produce confidently wrong answers.

rule.statement: Pre-register the analysis plan and segment cuts before fielding — define which comparisons you will make and at what N threshold per segment. Surveys without pre-registration become fishing expeditions.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc analysis finds patterns by chance; pre-registration forces honesty about what the survey is designed to answer.

rule.statement: Write neutral, non-hypothetical questions. Ask about past behaviour ("how often have you used...") not predictions ("would you use...").
rule.rationale: Hypothetical questions systematically overpredict positive intent; past behaviour is a stronger predictor of future behaviour.

rule.statement: One concept per question. Split double-barreled items into two separate questions.
rule.rationale: Double-barreled items produce uninterpretable responses: agreeing could mean agreeing with either clause or both.

rule.statement: Always include "Prefer not to say" or "Don't know" as response options. Forcing a choice corrupts data more than a missing response.
rule.rationale: Forced-choice on unknowable items produces random noise distributed across the scale that looks like signal.

rule.statement: Randomize answer order on multiple-choice questions (eliminates primacy/recency bias) but keep ordinal scales fixed in direction.
rule.rationale: Primacy and recency effects are large on long option lists; ordinal scales must be consistent to preserve comparability across respondents.

antipattern.description: Leading question: "Do you like our product?"
antipattern.reason: Positive framing biases toward agreement; rewrite as "How would you rate your experience with [product]?" on a 1-5 scale.

antipattern.description: Hypothetical: "How often will you use this feature?"
antipattern.reason: Predicts nothing; rewrite as "How often have you used similar features in other tools?"

section.title: Survey structure and length
section.p: Recommended flow: screener (1-2 qualifying questions) → easy openers (2-3 to build momentum) → core questions (5-10 main research) → demographics (3-5 for segmentation) → one open-end ("anything else?").

rule.statement: Target 5-7 minutes total length for standard research surveys; pilot with 5-10 people to verify timing and cut 25% of questions you thought were essential.
rule.rationale: Every minute above 7 minutes reduces completion rate by roughly 5-10%; abandonment at the end biases the completed sample toward highly engaged users.

rule.statement: Embed an attention check ("select 'agree' for this row") in any survey over 5 minutes; exclude respondents who fail.
rule.rationale: Inattentive respondents produce random noise; they are detectable and removable without compromising sample integrity.

rule.statement: Report margin of error explicitly per segment, not just overall N. Any segment with N below 30 gets descriptives only — no recommendations.
rule.rationale: Small segments reported as actionable findings are statistical noise dressed as insight.
